title: Elderflower and lemon cake
subtitle: A light Victoria-style sponge with elderflower cordial and lemon curd filling
excerpt: A two-layer lemon sponge moistened with elderflower cordial syrup, filled with lemon curd and elderflower cream, and finished with a thin elderflower icing. A seasonal cake for late May and June.
sourceNotes: Elderflower and lemon as a combination in baking is well established in British seasonal cooking. The elderflower sponge follows the Victoria sandwich tradition documented from the 1860s onwards (Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management, 1861, Project Gutenberg #10136). The use of elderflower cordial as both a flavouring and a soaking syrup reflects a technique common in contemporary British baking. Lemon curd as a cake filling is documented in early-twentieth-century British baking guides.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponge layers can be baked a day ahead and stored wrapped at room temperature. Assemble on the day.

glossaryTerms.definition: A concentrated syrup made from the flowers of the elder tree, sugar, and water, usually with lemon or citric acid. Widely available in British supermarkets from late spring. The season is brief; unopened cordial keeps for several months.

content.text: appears in three places in this cake: brushed onto the sponge layers as a soaking syrup, stirred into the cream filling, and thinned with lemon juice for the icing. Each layer reinforces the elderflower flavour without overpowering the lemon.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Grease and line two 20 cm round tins. Beat the butter and sugar until pale and fluffy. Beat in the eggs one at a time, then fold in the flour and lemon zest. Divide between the tins and bake for 25 to 30 minutes until golden and pulling away from the sides. While the cakes are still warm, brush the soaking syrup over both surfaces. Cool completely on racks.

Whip the double cream with the 20 ml elderflower cordial to soft peaks. Place the first sponge on a serving plate and spread the lemon curd over the top to within 1 cm of the edge. Spoon the elderflower cream over the lemon curd and spread gently, then place the second sponge on top, pressing very lightly.

Mix the sifted icing sugar with the elderflower cordial and lemon juice to a pourable icing. It should be thin enough to flow but not transparent. Pour over the top of the assembled cake and allow it to drip naturally over the edges. Leave to set for 30 minutes before cutting.
